refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement; it depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context beyond the FR itself. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; meeting the target is validated through acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved) during operation, computes energy_per_ton, and reports results in a format accessible to the Site Manager and System Engineer for comparison against the customer-defined E_target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log energy consumption data during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log work output (tonnage moved) data during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy_per_ton as the ratio of cumulative energy consumed to cumulative tonnage moved.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy_per_ton metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to onboard compute module; calculate energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; log for post-evaluation reporting.

DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage counting at load transfer point; synchronize timestamps; batch-calculate energy_per_ton at shift/day boundaries; store in local database; export for compliance review.

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline via calibrated measurement during controlled test cycle; apply linear scaling factor to operational tonnage; report scaled energy_per_ton without continuous measurement.

DP5: Decompose energy consumption into sub-processes (e.g., propulsion, lifting, auxiliary); measure each independently; weight by operational duty cycle; synthesize composite energy_per_ton metric; report with process-level transparency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage counting at load transfer point; synchronize timestamps; batch-calculate energy_per_ton at shift/day boundaries; store in local database; export for compliance review.
selected_dp.rationale_for_selection: DP3 balances measurement rigor (independent instrumentation of energy and work), operational practicality (batch calculation avoids real-time compute overhead), and architectural independence (energy and tonnage measurement are decoupled at source, reducing sensor fusion complexity). It supports the acceptance criterion directly and aligns with worksite operational cadence (shift/day reporting). DP1 is too coarse (offline-only, no operational visibility). DP2 introduces unnecessary real-time compute coupling and alert logic outside the FR scope. DP4 sacrifices measurement fidelity via scaling assumptions. DP5 adds process-level decomposition not required by the FR and increases instrumentation complexity.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage subsystems: if clocks drift, energy_per_ton ratio becomes unreliable. Mitigation: enforce synchronized time source (NTP or GPS) at both measurement points.

Data pipeline coupling: if energy metering and tonnage counting share a single communication bus or database write path, failure in one affects reporting of both. Mitigation: use independent data channels (separate CAN, Modbus, or wireless links) with local buffering.

Batch calculation window definition: if shift/day boundary is ambiguous (e.g., overlapping shifts, partial days), energy_per_ton becomes ambiguous. Mitigation: define boundary rules explicitly in operational policy; enforce in calculation logic.

Target threshold definition: if E_target is not clearly specified in contract or changes mid-evaluation, acceptance becomes subjective. Mitigation: lock E_target at evaluation start; document in compliance record.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 avoids tight coupling between measurement and control logic (unlike DP2). Energy and tonnage are measured at independent physical points, reducing sensor fusion risk. Batch calculation decouples real-time operational control from compliance reporting, lowering architectural complexity. Primary risk is data synchronization and boundary definition, both addressable via operational policy and software design discipline.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy metering hardware (fuel flow meter, electrical power analyzer) is available and can be integrated at the power distribution node without disrupting equipment operation. RISK: If integration requires equipment downtime or is mechanically infeasible, DP3 becomes impractical. Mitigation: conduct feasibility study on target equipment before design freeze.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage counting can be performed at a single load transfer point (e.g., discharge chute, conveyor exit) with sufficient accuracy and without double-counting. RISK: If material is transferred multiple times or bypasses the measurement point, tonnage metric becomes invalid. Mitigation: map material flow paths; validate that all work output passes through instrumented point.

ASSUMPTION: Shift/day boundaries are operationally well-defined and consistently enforced. RISK: If operations are continuous or shifts overlap, batch window becomes ambiguous, leading to disputes over energy_per_ton calculation. Mitigation: define boundary rules in operational policy; enforce via automated timestamp logic.

ASSUMPTION: Local database storage is reliable and data export is tamper-evident (e.g., cryptographic signing or audit trail). RISK: If data can be modified post-collection, compliance reporting loses credibility. Mitigation: implement write-once storage or immutable log; include audit trail in export.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is fixed for the evaluation period and is technically achievable with the equipment as-is. RISK: If target is unrealistic or changes mid-evaluation, acceptance becomes impossible or disputed. Mitigation: validate target against baseline performance; document in contract with change-control process.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation of energy and tonnage sensors without introducing new hazards. RISK: If sensors create pinch points, electrical hazards, or obstruct operator visibility, safety approval may be withheld. Mitigation: involve safety officer in sensor placement design; conduct HAZOP review.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to average out operational variability and weather effects. RISK: If evaluation is too short, energy_per_ton may be skewed by atypical conditions (e.g., single heavy load day). Mitigation: define minimum evaluation duration in contract; collect baseline statistics to estimate required period.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved) over a defined evaluation period, calculated at shift/day boundaries, stored in a tamper-evident local database, and exported for compliance review against a customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target. Measurement shall be independent at energy source (power distribution node) and work output point (load transfer point), with synchronized timestamps. Acceptance criterion: measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target, verified by third-party audit of exported data and operational logs, subject to worksite safety plan and operational policies. Evaluation period, E_target value, and batch boundary rules shall be locked in writing before evaluation start.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage accounting, timestamp synchronization, batch energy-per-ton calculation, local persistence, and compliance export orchestration.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive real-time power distribution node readings and load transfer point tonnage counts from Vehicle and distributed sensors.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Signal shift/day boundary events and trigger batch calculation cycles.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Export compliance-ready energy_per_ton reports to external systems or cloud repositories.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: metering, counting, synchronization, batch calculation, and local storage are all logically unified under energy/tonnage accounting. NGHE is the natural owner of compliance-grade energy metrics. Minor diffusion: Telemetry ingestion requires Vehicle interface; export may involve Network Link, but both are well-defined boundaries.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a mobile asset, not a stationary power distribution node. Energy metering and batch calculation belong at infrastructure level, not on-board. Tonnage counting at load transfer point is fixed-location, not vehicle-centric. Significant diffusion: calculation logic, local database, and compliance export would leak into NGHE Solution anyway.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface for remote operation, not a data aggregation or metering system. Energy accounting and compliance reporting are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: all core logic would reside elsewhere; Teleoperation would only consume reports.

Poor fit: Safety Services handles hazard detection and alerts, not energy/tonnage accounting. While energy efficiency could inform safety (e.g., overload detection), the primary DP is compliance metering, not safety. High diffusion: calculation and storage logic would not naturally belong in Safety Services; coupling would be weak and artificial.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of energy metering, tonnage accounting, and compliance reporting because it operates at the infrastructure/system level where power distribution nodes and load transfer points are managed. The DP's core logic—synchronization, batch calculation, and local persistence—is cohesive and isolated from vehicle dynamics, teleoperation commands, and safety alerts. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/Network Link out) are clean and minimal.
containment_risks: Telemetry ingestion from distributed sensors (power nodes, load points) must be reliable and timestamped at source; clock skew across nodes could undermine synchronization.
Local database schema and export format must be standardized to ensure compliance; changes to regulatory requirements may force DP revision.
Batch boundary definition (shift/day) is policy-driven; if boundaries change frequently, Tasking interface coupling may increase.
If Vehicle or Teleoperation systems require real-time energy feedback (e.g., for load balancing), additional Telemetry out-flow from NGHE Solution may emerge, increasing coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete measurement and reporting pipeline: energy metering at power distribution nodes, tonnage counting at load transfer points, timestamp synchronization, batch calculation of energy_per_ton, local persistence, and compliance export. The DP is concrete, measurable, and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry ingestion from distributed sensors (power nodes, load transfer points) introduces clock skew risk across geographically dispersed nodes; synchronization logic must enforce source-level timestamping and drift correction to maintain DP integrity.

Batch boundary definition (shift/day) is policy-driven and couples to Tasking interface; if operational boundaries change frequently (e.g., per-job accounting), DP revision and interface renegotiation will be required.

Local database schema and export format are compliance-critical; regulatory changes may force DP logic revision; schema versioning and backward compatibility must be designed into IM to avoid diffusion.

If Vehicle or Teleoperation systems require real-time energy feedback for load balancing or predictive maintenance, NGHE Solution will need to emit Telemetry out-flow, increasing coupling and potentially fragmenting calculation logic.

Export mechanism (Network Link) depends on external system availability and format compatibility; if multiple compliance frameworks are required, export logic may diffuse across multiple IM boundaries or require adapter modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the clear natural owner. Metering, counting, synchronization, batch calculation, and local storage are logically cohesive and operate at the infrastructure/system level where power distribution nodes and load transfer points are managed. Vehicle (0.35 containment) is a poor fit because it is mobile and does not own stationary metering points; Teleoperation (0.2 containment) is orthogonal to data aggregation; Safety Services (0.25 containment) is misaligned with compliance metering. External interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry in (from sensors), Tasking out (shift/day events), Network Link out (compliance reports). Minor diffusion exists only at interface boundaries, which is acceptable and expected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from IM outputs: local database records of energy_used and ton_moved, aggregated over the agreed evaluation period. Batch calculation at shift/day boundaries provides clear audit points. Compliance export provides traceability. Verification requires: (1) validation of metering accuracy at power distribution nodes, (2) validation of tonnage counting at load transfer points, (3) clock synchronization tolerance analysis, (4) batch calculation logic review, (5) export format compliance check.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated ownership of the entire DP. Responsibilities are partitioned as: (1) Telemetry interface receives raw sensor data from Vehicle and distributed infrastructure sensors; (2) NGHE Solution owns synchronization, batch calculation, and local persistence; (3) Tasking interface triggers batch boundaries; (4) Network Link exports compliance-ready reports. No ambiguity in module boundaries or responsibility handoff. Ownership is traceable to a single IM (NGHE Solution), reducing diffusion risk.

recommended_revisions: Specify clock synchronization protocol and tolerance thresholds in DP to mitigate distributed sensor clock skew; define acceptable drift (e.g., ±5 seconds) and correction mechanism (e.g., NTP, GPS time, or centralized time server).

Parameterize batch boundary definition (shift/day) in DP to allow policy-driven reconfiguration without DP logic revision; decouple boundary events from hardcoded schedules by routing through Tasking interface with explicit event payloads.
Define local database schema versioning and export format versioning strategy to isolate compliance-critical changes from DP logic; establish change control process for regulatory updates.

Add optional Telemetry out-flow from NGHE Solution to Vehicle/Teleoperation for real-time energy feedback (e.g., per-load energy intensity) if load balancing or predictive maintenance is a future requirement; document this as a potential extension to avoid surprise diffusion.

Specify export adapter pattern in IM to support multiple compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 50001, customer-specific targets) without fragmenting calculation logic; centralize format translation in a dedicated export module within NGHE Solution.

Establish acceptance test plan that validates: (1) metering accuracy within ±2% of reference standard, (2) tonnage counting accuracy within ±1%, (3) timestamp synchronization within specified tolerance, (4) batch calculation correctness against manual audit samples, (5) export format compliance against regulatory schema.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear allocation to NGHE Solution (0.92 containment). However, the gate analysis identifies six material diffusion risks and recommended revisions that require sub-level decomposition to address: (1) clock synchronization protocol and tolerance thresholds, (2) batch boundary parameterization and decoupling from hardcoded schedules, (3) database schema versioning and export format versioning, (4) optional real-time telemetry feedback out-flow, (5) export adapter pattern for multiple compliance frameworks, and (6) acceptance test plan validation. These are not design flaws but rather architectural details that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure DP robustness, IM modularity, and verification feasibility. Safety criticality (high) reinforces the need for explicit sub-level treatment of synchronization, metering accuracy, and audit traceability. Stopping here would leave critical implementation details unspecified and increase diffusion risk during realization.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Synchronize timestamps across distributed power metering and tonnage counting sensors to within acceptable clock drift tolerance, enabling accurate batch-level energy_per_ton calculation.

FR3.2: Parameterize batch boundary definition (shift/day/job) as a policy-driven configuration decoupled from hardcoded schedules, allowing reconfiguration via Tasking interface without DP logic revision.
FR3.3: Version local database schema and compliance export format independently to isolate regulatory changes from core calculation logic and maintain backward compatibility.
FR3.4: Translate batch-aggregated energy and tonnage data into multiple compliance-ready export formats (e.g., ISO 50001, customer-specific targets) without fragmenting calculation logic.

FR3.5: Provide optional real-time energy intensity feedback (energy_per_ton per load) to Vehicle and Teleoperation systems for load balancing and predictive maintenance, with clear interface contract and optional activation.

FR3.6: Validate metering accuracy, tonnage counting accuracy, timestamp synchronization, batch calculation correctness, and export format compliance against regulatory and operational acceptance criteria.

revise.why: The current DP3 is logically sound but lacks explicit specification of: (1) clock synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS, centralized time server) and tolerance thresholds (e.g., ±5 seconds); (2) batch boundary parameterization mechanism and Tasking interface contract; (3) schema versioning and export format versioning strategy; (4) export adapter pattern for multiple compliance frameworks; (5) optional telemetry out-flow contract and activation conditions. These details must be elevated to sub-level DPs to ensure IM can be designed with modularity, testability, and regulatory compliance. Revising DP3 now to include these specifications will prevent rework during detailed design and reduce diffusion risk.

assumptions: Clock synchronization tolerance of ±5 seconds is acceptable for batch-level energy_per_ton calculation; tighter tolerance (e.g., ±1 second) may require GPS or atomic clock synchronization, increasing cost.
Batch boundary events (shift/day/job) are triggered by Tasking interface with explicit event payloads; operational boundaries do not change more frequently than daily without prior notice.
Compliance export format is stable within a regulatory cycle (e.g., 12 months); regulatory changes are infrequent and managed through schema versioning.
Vehicle and Teleoperation systems do not require real-time energy feedback for critical control loops; optional telemetry out-flow is a future enhancement, not a current requirement.
Export adapter pattern can be implemented within NGHE Solution without requiring external adapter modules or third-party libraries.
Metering accuracy of ±2% and tonnage counting accuracy of ±1% are achievable with standard industrial sensors and calibration procedures.
open_risks: Clock skew across geographically dispersed sensors may exceed ±5 second tolerance if network latency is high or NTP synchronization is unreliable; mitigation requires GPS-based time synchronization or centralized time server with redundancy.

Batch boundary definition may become a bottleneck if operational requirements change frequently (e.g., per-job accounting instead of per-shift); parameterization must be flexible enough to support dynamic boundary reconfiguration without DP revision.

Compliance export format may need to support multiple regulatory frameworks simultaneously (e.g., ISO 50001, customer-specific targets, regional emissions standards); export adapter pattern must be extensible to avoid code duplication and maintenance burden.

Real-time energy feedback to Vehicle/Teleoperation may introduce coupling and increase IM complexity; optional activation mechanism must be clearly defined to avoid surprise diffusion during integration.
Database schema versioning and export format versioning may introduce backward compatibility issues if not carefully managed; change control process must be established and enforced.

Acceptance test plan must validate metering accuracy, tonnage counting accuracy, and timestamp synchronization under realistic operational conditions (e.g., high-load scenarios, network congestion, sensor failures); lab testing alone may not be sufficient.
evidence_needed: Clock synchronization protocol specification with tolerance thresholds, drift correction mechanism, and validation procedure (e.g., NTP accuracy analysis, GPS time availability, centralized time server design).

Batch boundary parameterization design with Tasking interface contract, event payload schema, and reconfiguration procedure (e.g., configuration file format, API specification, change notification mechanism).

Database schema versioning and export format versioning strategy with change control process, backward compatibility rules, and migration procedure (e.g., schema evolution rules, export format versioning scheme, deprecation policy).

Export adapter pattern design with support for multiple compliance frameworks, adapter interface specification, and extensibility mechanism (e.g., adapter registry, plugin architecture, format translation rules).
Optional telemetry out-flow specification with interface contract, activation conditions, and integration points (e.g., Vehicle/Teleoperation API, real-time data format, subscription mechanism).

Acceptance test plan with test cases for metering accuracy (±2%), tonnage counting accuracy (±1%), timestamp synchronization (±5 seconds), batch calculation correctness, and export format compliance; test data, reference standards, and validation procedures.
